name: Hills and Valleys of Chalosse Loop
name_original: Coteaux et vallons de Chalosse — boucle
description: A challenging 42.1-mile touring cycling loop through the rolling, green landscapes of the Chalosse region, with 2248 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Montfort-En-Chalosse traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills and fertile valleys, typical of the Chalosse region. The area features a mix of agricultural land, small woodlands, and river plains, offering varied terrain for cyclists. Routes often follow quiet country roads and sections of greenways, providing a blend of gentle gradients and more challenging ascents. Best touring cycling routes around Montfort-En-Chalosse The most popular touring cycling route is Hills and Valleys of Chalosse Loop, a 42.1 miles (67.7 km) trail that takes 4 hours 16 minutes to complete. This route explores the varied topography of the Chalosse region, featuring numerous ascents and descents. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is The Hot Fountain – Presidents' Footbridge loop from Lourquen, a moderate 33.1 miles (53.2 km) path. This route includes sections along the Adour river and passes through local villages. Local touring cyclists also love the The Arena in Pontonx-sur-l'Adour – The Adour and the Barthes loop from Cassen, a 37.5 miles (60.4 km) trail leading through river plains and agricultural areas, often completed in about 3 hours 31 minutes. Touring cycling around Montfort-En-Chalosse is defined by rolling hills, river valleys, and sections of the voie verte de Chalosse. The network offers options for easy bike trails as well as advanced cycling routes. The routes in Montfort-En-Chalosse are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 30 reviews. More than 200 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Montfort-En-Chalosse's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Montfort-En-Chalosse?
A: There are over 60 touring cycling routes around Montfort-En-Chalosse, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists of all levels. These include 21 easy routes, 32 moderate routes, and 11 more challenging options.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Montfort-En-Chalosse offers several easy touring cycling routes. The region's greenways, such as sections of the Voie verte de Chalosse, provide gentle gradients ideal for beginners and families. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20638637" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Chalosse Viewpoint – Voie verte de Chalosse loop from Montfort-en-Chalosse&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 32.9 km route that incorporates parts of the greenway, offering a pleasant ride.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Montfort-En-Chalosse?
A: The terrain around Montfort-En-Chalosse is characterized by rolling hills and fertile valleys, typical of the Chalosse region. You'll encounter a mix of agricultural land, small woodlands, and river plains. Routes often follow quiet country roads and sections of greenways, providing a blend of gentle gradients and more challenging ascents.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes around Montfort-En-Chalosse are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24831648" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Hot Fountain – Presidents' Footbridge loop from Lourquen&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 53.2 km path that brings you back to your starting point.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or landmarks I can visit while cycling?
A: While cycling, you can discover several scenic spots and landmarks. The region features viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5789369" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Chalosse Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6095647" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Montfort-en-Chalosse viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic views of the landscape. You might also pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1018005" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Voie Verte de Chalosse Railway Tunnel&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6073541" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pomarez Arenas&lt;/a&gt;, which are notable local attractions.

Q: Are there any advanced or challenging touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, for experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, there are routes with more significant ascents and descents. The region offers 11 difficult routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1286765908/hills-and-valleys-of-chalosse-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hills and Valleys of Chalosse Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 67.7 km trail that explores the varied topography of the Chalosse region with numerous climbs.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Montfort-En-Chalosse?
A: The spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant conditions for touring cycling in Montfort-En-Chalosse, with milder temperatures and less rainfall. Summer can be warm, but early mornings or late afternoons are still enjoyable. Winter cycling is possible, but be prepared for cooler, potentially damp weather.

Q: Can I access touring cycling routes using public transport?
A: While Montfort-En-Chalosse is primarily accessed by car, some local bus services connect the villages. However, public transport options for reaching specific trailheads with a bicycle might be limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules and bicycle carriage policies in advance if you plan to rely on public transport.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes?
A: Parking is generally available in and around the main villages and towns, such as Montfort-en-Chalosse itself, or starting points for specific routes like Cassen or Lourquen. Look for designated public parking areas, often near town centers or local amenities, which typically serve as convenient starting points for rides.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Montfort-En-Chalosse?
A: The touring cycling routes in Montfort-En-Chalosse are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 30 reviews. Cyclists often praise the quiet country roads, the varied landscape of rolling hills and river valleys, and the well-maintained sections of the &lt;em&gt;voie verte&lt;/em&gt;.

Q: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes pass through or near charming villages where you can find local cafes, bakeries, or small shops to refuel and take a break. Planning your route to include stops in towns like Cassen or Pontonx-sur-l'Adour, as seen on routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40126112" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Arena in Pontonx-sur-l'Adour – The Adour and the Barthes loop from Cassen&lt;/a&gt;, is a good way to ensure refreshment stops.

Q: What is the 'Voie verte de Chalosse' and how does it relate to touring cycling?
A: The 'Voie verte de Chalosse' is a greenway, a dedicated path for non-motorized transport, often built on disused railway lines. It provides a safe and pleasant cycling experience with gentle gradients, making it ideal for touring cyclists, especially families or those seeking a more relaxed ride. Several touring routes in the region incorporate sections of this greenway, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20638837" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mugron area – Voie verte de Chalosse loop from Cassen&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: The Chalosse greenway in the Landes is a former railway line redeveloped into a pedestrian route. Covered with gravel, it is used for running, mountain biking or horse riding.
From this belvedere you will have a breathtaking view of the Chalosse below. The gazebo next to it can offer you the perfect shelter in case of rain.

In Chalosse, in the heart of the hilly Landes, the Moulin de Poyaller welcomes everyone, families, children's groups, adult groups, for a bucolic walk. The century-old trees and the river keep things cool on hot days. The boat trip on the water combines a refreshing atmosphere and a privileged natural environment, through animal parks.

Romanesque building with circular apse and rectangular bell tower, of which only the apse remains, dating from the end of the 12th century. Inside, you can see 18th century woodwork, a monumental 17th century wooden chandelier, funerary slabs, and above all a marble high altar made around 1770 by the Mazzetti sculptors of Swiss origin who worked also in Laurède, Montaut, Aire sur l'Adour, Brocas les Forges, Mont de Marsan, Saint Pierre du Mont, Dax, Orthevielle, Pouillon and Sorde l'Abbaye.

Built in cement in 1931 and covered in 1958, the Pomarez arenas are among the first completely covered in France. They host around fifteen bullfighting events each year, mainly Landes races, in addition to regular training from the Pomarez bullfighting school, the only one to teach this typically Landes game. The Pentecost races, those on the 1st Sunday of August and those on August 15 are benchmarks in the Landes bullfighting season.
These arenas helped give Pomarez its nickname “Mecca of Landes racing”.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Coteaux et vallons de Chalosse loop?
faqs.answer: This cycling loop is classified as difficult by komoot, requiring very good fitness. While the region is generally accessible, the route features significant elevation gain (685 meters) over its 67.7-kilometer distance, making it a challenging ride. Expect to be cycling for around 4 hours and 16 minutes.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The route primarily consists of paved surfaces, with approximately 37.8 km of asphalt and 24.1 km of paved roads. You will also encounter smaller sections of compacted gravel (5.12 km) and some unpaved paths (248 m). The loop mainly follows roads, with some segments on trails, streets, access roads, and cycle paths.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Coteaux et vallons de Chalosse loop?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you can discover several interesting spots. Keep an eye out for the Voie Verte de Chalosse Railway Tunnel, enjoy the views from The Chalosse Viewpoint, and pass by the historic Poyaller Mill. You'll also see the Saint-Martin Church of Caupenne and the Pomarez Arenas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Chalosse region offers pleasant cycling experiences even in less-than-ideal weather, thanks to its rolling, green landscapes. However, for the most comfortable ride, spring and autumn generally provide milder temperatures and beautiful scenery, avoiding the peak heat of summer.

faqs.question: Is the Coteaux et vallons de Chalosse loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route primarily uses roads and paved surfaces, which are generally accessible, specific regulations for dogs on cycling paths in the Landes region can vary. It's always best to keep dogs on a leash, especially when cycling on shared paths or near roads. Consider the length and difficulty of the route when bringing a dog.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Coteaux et vallons de Chalosse loop. It is a public route accessible to cyclists.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Coteaux et vallons de Chalosse loop?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact loop are not detailed, you can typically find parking in the towns and villages along the route, such as Montfort-en-Chalosse, Sort-en-Chalosse, or Saint-Sever. Look for public parking lots in these areas to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Does this cycling route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Coteaux et vallons de Chalosse loop incorporates sections of several other official trails. Notably, it largely follows the 'Coteaux et vallons de Chalosse' trail itself (92% of the route). You will also find segments of the 'Tour de la Haute Chalosse' and the 'Voie Verte de Chalosse'.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or drinks along the way?
faqs.answer: The Chalosse region is known for its charming villages. While specific cafes or pubs directly on the route are not listed, you will pass through or near several towns where you can find local establishments to refuel and refresh. Consider Montfort-en-Chalosse, Sort-en-Chalosse, or Saint-Sever for options.

faqs.question: What kind of bike is best suited for this loop?
faqs.answer: Given that the route is primarily paved (asphalt and paved roads) with some sections of compacted gravel, a touring bicycle is ideal. A road bike with slightly wider tires could also work, but a touring or gravel bike would offer more comfort and stability on the varied surfaces.

faqs.question: Is this loop suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Due to its 'difficult' rating, significant distance (67.7 km), and elevation gain (685 m), this loop is generally not recommended for families with young children or beginner cyclists. It requires a very good fitness level and is better suited for experienced riders seeking a challenge.
